coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of luminescent TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ encapsulated PLGA microparticles for bioimaging application with enhanced biocompatibility
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Chaohui
Teng, Choon Peng
Yang, Da-Peng
Lin, Ming
Win, Khin Yin
Li, Zibiao
Ye, Enyi
coredata.dc:description: Rare earth is of great interest because of their unique optical properties, especially the rich luminescent spectra. In this study, we developed a facile one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis of luminescent Eu3+ doped TiO2 nanoparticles and Tb3+ doped ZrO2 nanoparticles. As a result, the emitting centers (Eu3+ and Tb3+) were all well dispersed in the amorphous host oxide materials, leading to high luminescence. The obtained TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles were then encapsulated into PLGA microparticles for bio-applications. These luminescent microparticles were then proven to be highly stable, biocompatible and of low cytotoxicity. We successfully demonstrated the bioimaging of live cells using the red-luminescent TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles and green-luminescent ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles embedded PLGA microparticles. The microwave-assisted synthetic methodology can be further developed to be general method to prepare oxide nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave-assisted solvothermal treatment
Rare earth doping
Luminescent nanoparticles
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Eu3+ doped TiO2 nanoparticles
Preparation of Tb3+ doped ZrO2 nanoparticles
Preparation of luminescent (TiO2:Eu3+, ZrO2:Tb3+) nanoparticles incorporated PLGA microparticles
Preparation and characterization of TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles
In vitro cytotoxicity of TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ incorporated PLGA microparticles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81472001, 31400851), the Minjiang Scholars Program of Fujian Province, the Tongjiang Scholars Program of Quanzhou City, the Fourth Health Education Joint Development Project of Fujian Province (WKJ-2016-2-36).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electron Microscopy images of the as-prepared TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles. (A) STEM image of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles, (B) TEM image of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles, (C) STEM image of ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles and (D) TEM image of ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles.

Characteristic optical spectra of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles. (A) Excitation spectrum with λem =615nm, (B) Luminescent emission spectra of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles measured under excitation from 338 to 536nm. Inset: photograph taken under 365nm UV irradiation.

Characteristic optical spectra of ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles. (A) Excitation spectrum with λem =545nm, (B) luminescent emission spectra of ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles measured under excitation from 305 to 488nm. Inset: photograph taken under 365nm UV irradiation.
Viability of MCF-7 cancer cells incubated with TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ incorporated PLGA microparticles at different concentrations.

(A) Confocal luminescent microscope image of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles incorporated PLGA microparticles excited with 405nm laser light; (B) SEM image of the TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles incorporated PLGA microparticles; (C) confocal luminescent microscope image of ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles incorporated PLGA microparticles excited with 488nm laser light; (D) SEM image of the ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles incorporated PLGA microparticles.

Confocal microscopy images of MCF-7 human breast cancer cells (A, D) transmission images of MCF-7 cells; (B, C) luminescent image and overlapped image of MCF-7 cells associated with TiO2:Eu3+ embedded PLGA microparticles with excitation wavelength λex =405nm, emission collected in 570–650nm region; (E, F) luminescent image and overlapped image of MCF-7 cells associated with ZrO2:Tb3+ embedded PLGA microparticles with excitation wavelength λex =488nm, emission collected in 500–600nm; the nucleus were stained with DAPI with excitation wavelength λex =405nm, emission collected in 420–500nm region. (For interpretation of the references to color in this legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of emulsion solvent evaporation method for encapsulation of TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles into PLGA microparticles.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of luminescent TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ encapsulated PLGA microparticles for bioimaging application with enhanced biocompatibility

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Quanzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering & Materials Science, Quanzhou Normal University, Quanzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering & Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, 2 Fusionopolis Way, Innovis, #8-03, Singapore 138634, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: D.-P. Yang, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Quanzhou, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rare earth is of great interest because of their unique optical properties, especially the rich luminescent spectra. In this study, we developed a facile one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis of luminescent Eu3+ doped TiO2 nanoparticles and Tb3+ doped ZrO2 nanoparticles. As a result, the emitting centers (Eu3+ and Tb3+) were all well dispersed in the amorphous host oxide materials, leading to high luminescence. The obtained TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles were then encapsulated into PLGA microparticles for bio-applications. These luminescent microparticles were then proven to be highly stable, biocompatible and of low cytotoxicity. We successfully demonstrated the bioimaging of live cells using the red-luminescent TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles and green-luminescent ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles embedded PLGA microparticles. The microwave-assisted synthetic methodology can be further developed to be general method to prepare oxide nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:para: Materials with nanoscale dimensions are of great interest to the researchers globally [1–13]. In the past three decades, significant progresses have been achieved in the synthetic methodology of nanomaterials [14–24], the unveiling of the structure-property relationship [25,26], and the applications of functional nanomaterials etc. [27–34] Unlike their bulk counterparts, based on their size, morphology and compositions, nanomaterials always exhibit unique properties, such as catalytic, plasmonic, magnetic, luminescent, electrical properties etc. In recent years, functional nanomaterials such as metallic nanocrystals [2,5,9,22,35–38], semiconducting materials, luminescent nanocrystals [39–47], metal oxides [18], carbon based materials [48–55] (e.g. carbon dots, CNT, graphene) attract great attention in the research society. A lot of efforts have been invested in exploiting the biomedical applications of such functional nanomaterials. Among which, the luminescent nanomaterials including quantum dots [56,57], rare earth based materials [39–47,58–61], carbon dots [48–54] and aggregation-induced-emission (AIE) [62,63] materials receive continuous focus because of their promising applications in bioimaging, diagnostics and sensing etc. Owing to their particular electronic structure, the rare earth (RE) elements possess unique spectroscopic properties, the luminescence from the RE elements cover almost the full spectrum from UV, visible to near infrared. The non-fully filled electron configuration of 4f5d which is shielded by the outer valence shells, endows the RE rich electron energy levels, leading to thousands of channels for transitions between different energy levels, bringing in rich spectrum upon external excitation [64]. However, due to the parity-forbidden f orbital transitions, direct excitation of the RE ions is not efficient. Host materials are thus required for efficient excitation of RE ions in a wide range. Inorganic metal oxides with good chemical, thermal and physical properties are ideal for host matrix.

Herein, we report a facile microwave-assisted solvothermal method to prepared Eu3+-doped TiO2 nanoparticles and Tb3+-doped ZrO2 nanoparticles. TiO2 and ZrO2 were chosen to replace the commonly used hosting matrix such Y2O3 and Gd2O3 which are of high cost. The obtained TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles were characterized with transmission electron microscope (TEM) to study their size and morphology. Amorphous monodispersed quasispherical nanoparticles were obtained for both cases. The luminescent properties were studied using fluorospectrometer, the results show that TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles are highly luminescent with red and green phosphorescence respectively. Prior to any biomedical applications, these luminescent nanoparticles were encapsulated into biodegradable polymer [28,65,66] to enhance the biocompatibility and lower any possible cytotoxicity. This was confirmed by viability test using alamarBlue assay. Furthermore, the application of these luminescent RE based nanoparticles for live cell bioimaging was successfully demonstrated.

Europium (III) acetate (Eu(CH3CO2)3, 99.9%), terbium (III) acetate (Tb(CH3CO2)3, 99.9%) titanium (IV) butoxide (TBOT, 97%), zirconium (IV) propoxide (70% in 1-propanol), oleic acid (90%), absolute ethanol (99%), n-hexane (95%), poly (d,l-lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) (with L:G molar ratio of 50:50, and Mw of 30–60k), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (Mw 30–70k), phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH7.4), DMEM culture medium, penicillin-streptomycin solution, and Trypsin-EDTA solution were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Dichloromethane (DCM, analytical grade), and fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Merck, Fisher Scientific, and Gibco, respectively. DAPI and propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from Molecular Probes. All chemicals were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Eu3+ doped TiO2 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, 1mmol Europium acetate was dissolved in 15mL oleic acid at 100°C under vacuum to form a clear solution and to remove the water content. Then 2mmol (~0.7mL) titanium butoxide (TBOT 97%) were added into the above solution under vigorous stirring. The resulting transparent solution was transferred to a 30-mL reaction vessel, which was then sealed with septa and placed into an Anton Parr Monowave 300 microwave reactor. The solution was quickly heated to 260°C within ~3.5min, the reaction temperature maintained at 260°C for another 1h. The reaction was stopped by cooling down with pressurized air flow. The obtained mixture was then diluted with hexane and precipitated by adding ethanol followed by centrifugation at 10000rpm for 10min. This process was repeated twice to purify the Eu3+ doped TiO2 nanoparticles, and the final product was dispersed in dichloromethane for further usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Tb3+ doped ZrO2 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to preparation of Eu3+ doped TiO2 nanoparticles, experimentally, 1mmol Terbium acetate was first dissolved in 15mL oleic acid at 100°C under vacuum to form a clear solution and to remove the water content. Then 2mmol Zirconium propoxide were added and the whole solution was loaded into a 30-mL reaction vessel, which was then sealed with septa and placed into the Anton Parr Monowave 300 microwave reactor. The mixture was heated to 260°C within 3.5min; the temperature was maintained for 1h. The reaction was stopped by cooling down with pressurized air flow. The product was then purified by washing with mixed solvent of hexane and ethanol followed by centrifugation at 10000rpm for 10min. The final product was dispersed in dichloromethane for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of luminescent (TiO2:Eu3+, ZrO2:Tb3+) nanoparticles incorporated PLGA microparticles
ce:section.ce:para: After purification, TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles were dispersed in DCM and mixed with DCM solution of PLGA. As is illustrated in Scheme 1 , emulsion solvent evaporation method with PVA as the emulsifier was used to prepare the rare earth doped luminescent nanoparticles-incorporated PLGA microparticles. In a typical process, 5mL DCM solution of nanoparticles (~30mg) and PLGA (~75mg) was introduced drop-wisely into 30mL aqueous PVA solution with concentration of 2wt% under stirring. The final mixture was then emulsified for 2min using an IKA homogenizer. The luminescent nanoparticles-incorporated PLGA microparticles were successfully recovered by evaporation of DCM from oil-in-water (O/W) droplets. The water-dispersible PLGA microparticles were further purified by washing with DI water followed by centrifugation 4 times to remove the excessive PVA. The purified PLGA microparticles were dispersed in DI water and kept in fridge for further use.

The luminescent spectra of TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles dispersed in DCM were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorospectrometer. The obtained luminescent rare earth doped oxide nanoparticles were studied with a FEI Titan TEM using both Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy (STEM) mode and TEM mode. TEM samples were prepared by casting a drop of diluted nanoparticles dispersion onto carbon coated copper grids followed by drying under ambient condition. Size and morphology of the TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles-incorporated PLGA microparticles were investigated using a JEOL JSM 5600LV scanning electron microscope (SEM). The samples were prepared by putting the microparticles on a metal stud with double-sided conductive tape and coated with gold using a JEOL JFC-1300 sputter coater. The average size and size distribution of the microparticles were determined by analyzing the SEM images with SmileView software. The luminescent imaging was carried out with a Leica TCS SP5 X confocal microscope.

Viability test of TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticle-incorporated PLGA microparticles was performed on MCF cancer cells using alamarBlue assay. Briefly, MCF cancer cells were first seeded with a density of 2.0×104 cells/cm2 into black 96-well plate and incubated overnight at 37.0°C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 [25]. The cells were incubated with fresh medium with various concentrations of the PLGA microparticles in five replicates on the next day. With 24h incubation, after washing with PBS, 100μL alamarBlue assay with concentration of 0.1mg/mL was added to each well to evaluate the viability of the cells in each well. The viable cells convert the resazurin (with blue emission) in alamarBlue assay into resorufin with red luminescent emission, which is measured with a Tecan microplate reader. The luminescent emissions at 595nm of the both the samples with PLGA microparticles (ODs) and the control without PLGA microparticles (ODc) were obtained to calculate the viability using the following formula:
Viable cells
% =
OD s
/
OD c
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles is based on a modified nonhydrolytic sol-gel ester elimination reaction using oleic acid as the solvent, stabilizing agent and hydrolysis agent, titanium butoxide as the titanium precursor, europium acetate as the europium precursor. As is reported, crystalline TiO2 nanorods were obtained by combination of oleic acid and TBOT at high temperature, which resulted in ester elimination reaction to produce TiO species. However, amorphous nanoparticles were obtained in our reaction system. The STEM image shown in Fig. 1A indicated the formation of quasispherical nanoparticles with average diameter of ~3nm. From the TEM image with higher magnification (Fig. 1B), no lattice fridges can be found on the nanoparticles, indicating the obtained nanoparticles are amorphous. It is believed that europium-oleic acid complex formed at 100°C by dissolving europium acetate precursor in oleic acid, and was incorporated into TiO species formed at the temperature 260°C. The Eu incorporated TiO species are not pure TiO system, it won't follow the anisotropic growth mechanism of TiO2 nanorods in the reaction of TBOT and oleic acid, thus quasispherical morphology was preferred to reduce the surface energy to be stable. The formation of ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles is similar to that of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles, the incorporation of Tb in to ZrO species resulted in amorphous nanoparticles. As is shown Fig. 1C, the obtained nanoparticles are quasispherical, and the HRTEM image (Fig. 1D) showed no lattice fridge for the nanoparticles suggesting the ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles are amorphous.

In the excitation spectrum, a transition can be observed only when the energy level is efficient in exciting the emitting level and in generating luminescence. The excitation spectrum of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles is recorded by monitoring the luminescence intensity as a function of excitation wavelengths, i.e. by fixing λem at 615nm and scanning the excitation wavelength from 220 to 600nm. As is shown in Fig. 2A, a few peaks were observed in the excitation spectrum of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles. By calculation based on the energy levels of the Eu3+ [67], the corresponding transition of each peak in the excitation spectrum can be identified, with 536nm for the 5D1 ← 7F1 transition, 525nm for the 5D1 ← 7F0 transition, 466nm for the 5D2 ← 7F0 transition, 416nm for the 5D3 ← 7F1 transition, 395nm for the 5D6 ← 7F0 transition, 382nm for the 5G2 ← 7F0 transition, 362nm for the 5D4 ← 7F0 transition. The peak 338nm can't be identified with any transition from Eu3+, while it could be from the surface state from the amorphous TiO2 [68]. The luminescent spectra of TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles were recorded with different excitation wavelengths, the luminescence peaks associated with Eu3+ f orbital transitions from emitting level of 5D0 to the 7FJ ground level were observed. The strongest emission peak at 615nm is attributed to the 5D0 → 7F2 (forced electric dipole transition), and 592nm from the allowed magnetic dipole transition of 5D0 → 7F1. The weak peak at 580nm can be attributed to 5D0 → 7F0 transition. The noticeable inhomogeneous broadening of the luminescence bands is also a strong indication of the distribution of Eu3+ ions in an amorphous oxide environment [69].

The excitation spectrum of ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles is recorded by monitoring the luminescence intensity as a function of excitation wavelengths, i.e. by fixing λem at 545nm and scanning the excitation wavelength from 220 to 525nm. The observed peaks in the excitation spectrum (Fig. 3A) can be attributed to the either f-d or f-f orbital transitions from Tb3+, with 488nm attributed to 5D4 ← 7F6 transition, 380nm to 5D3 ← 7F6, 371nm to 5 L10 ← 7F6, 353nm to 5 L9 ← 7F6, 320nm to 5H7 ← 7F6, 305nm to 5H6 ← 7F6, 259nm to 5 K9 ← 7F6. The luminescence peaks shown in Fig. 3B are associated with transitions from the emitting level 5D4 to the ground level of 7FJ, in detail, 490nm is attributed to 5D4 ← 7F6, 545nm to 5D4 ← 7F5, 585nm to 5D4 ← 7F4. It is to say under different excitation wavelength, the Tb3+ will be excited to different energy levels, and then relaxed to the emitting level of 5D4 via non-radiative transition, eventually to the ground level via radiative transition to emit luminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ incorporated PLGA microparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Biocompatibility is the fundamental requirement for any materials that is to be used for any biomedical related applications. The as prepared TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles were with hydrophobic surfaces, making them not suitable for bio-applications. PLGA as an amphiphilic biodegradable polymer will undergo hydrolysis in the body to produce its original monomers of lactic acid and glycolic acid, making it ideal for encapsulation of hydrophobic nanoparticles. After the TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles were encapsulated into PLGA microparticles, their biocompatibility on MCF-7 cancer cells was tested using alamarBlue assay. The results of viability test with different concentration of microparticles were shown in Fig. 4 . It is obvious that the encapsulation in PLGA makes the luminescent rare earth doped nanoparticles highly biocompatible.

As is shown above, both TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles exhibit promising luminescent properties. After encapsulation with PLGA, these luminescent nanoparticles gained high biocompatibility with lower cytotoxicity, making them ready for bioimaging applications. Before incubation with living cells, the luminescent properties of these TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ incorporated PLGA microparticles were evaluated using confocal microscope. Under the excitation of 405nm laser light, the TiO2:Eu3+ incorporated PLGA microparticles turned to be bright red (Fig. 5A), the size of the microparticles observed under confocal microscope is consistent with what was observed in SEM (Fig. 5B). With irradiation of 488nm laser light, the ZrO2:Tb3+ incorporated PLGA microparticles were found to be green (Fig. 5C), with the size in agreement with what was observed in their SEM image (Fig. 5D). From both figures, it can be clearly seen that TiO2:Eu3+ and ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles were homogeneously distributed in PLGA; what's more, the encapsulation with PLGA didn't affect the luminescent properties.

To carry out cell imaging using either TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles incorporated PLGA microparticles or ZrO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles incorporated PLGA microparticles, MCF-7 human breast cancer cells were seeded on an 8-well chambered coverglass and incubated with medium overnight at 37°C. On the next day, TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ incorporated PLGA microparticles with concentration of 250μg/mL were added to each well, followed by incubation at 37°C for 4h to enable the cells interacting with microparticles which eventually were associated with the cells. Pre-warmed PBS was used to rinse the cells for at least three times in order to remove the non-associated microparticles. A Leica TCS SP5 X confocal microscope system was used to perform the imaging of the live cells. MCF-7 cells incubated with TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles incorporated PLGA microparticles were viewed under the excitation of 405nm laser light. From the transmission images of the MCF-7 cancer cells (Fig. 6A and D), the efficient uptake of microparticles in the cytoplasm of the cells can be clearly observed. As can be seen in Fig. 6B and E, the cytoplasm of MCF-7 cells were loaded with microparticles and labeled with luminescence from TiO2:Eu3+ (red) and ZrO2:Tb3+ (green) particles, respectively. Fig. 6C and F illustrates the transmission/luminescence overlay images of the MCF-7 cells showing the uptake of microparticles and labeling of the cells. In both cases, proliferation of the cells was not affected by the treatment with microparticles. It is also worth to note that under the confocal laser irradiation neither photo-bleaching nor blinking were observed, strongly indicating the high stability of these luminescent nanoparticles. Thus these luminescent TiO2:Eu3+/ZrO2:Tb3+ incorporated PLGA microparticles are biocompatible with low or no cytotoxicity and suitable for both in vitro and in vivo bioimaging applications.

In summary, we have successfully developed a facile one-pot microwave-assisted synthetic method to prepare luminescent rare earth doped metal oxide nanoparticles. In the amorphous TiO2:Eu3+ nanoparticles, the amorphous TiO2 provides an ideal host matrix for well dispersion of the Eu3+ ions which significantly decreases unnecessary interaction among the Eu3+ resulting in high luminescence with red color. At the same time, the amorphous TiO2 also functions as sensitizer to absorb light and transfer energy to the emitting centers of Eu3+ ions. In a similar manner, Tb3+ ions were dispersed in the amorphous ZrO2, exhibiting green light luminescence. Both types of nanoparticles were encapsulated into PLGA microparticles respectively to make them biocompatible, which are proved by the viability test, paving the way for them to be used for bio-applications. Based on the excellent luminescence and biocompatibility, we have successfully demonstrated bioimaging of living cells using these RE doped nanoparticles. We believe that these luminescent RE doped nanophosphors can be further explored for applications of sensing, light emitting devices and display etc.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81472001, 31400851), the Minjiang Scholars Program of Fujian Province, the Tongjiang Scholars Program of Quanzhou City, the Fourth Health Education Joint Development Project of Fujian Province (WKJ-2016-2-36).
